narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 stood out as a strong point in your law school application, actually higher than the average of 3.24 among students who went on to pass the bar exam on their first try—this was the most influential factor pulling the model's prediction toward expecting you to succeed. However, your placement in the second decile for grades in your third year of school was a significant concern, as it pushed the prediction more toward the risk of failing the bar, especially compared to the typical fifth or sixth decile rank for those who passed. Your first-year decile ranking of second also contributed somewhat to that risk, deviating from the stronger sixth-decile average seen in successful bar takers. Overall, the model estimated a 44.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which crossed the threshold used by the admissions team to reject applications aimed at prioritizing students likely to pass and practice law effectively. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of bar passers and your LSAT score of 30.50 reflects solid preparation, and pursuing full-time studies matches the path of nearly all who succeed, these elements weren't enough to offset the concerns from your school deciles. The model focuses on patterns from past students' academic trajectories to forecast bar success, and in your case, the year-three performance ranked as the second-biggest driver after your GPA's positive boost. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors here—but a data-driven signal that your in-school rankings suggest hurdles similar to others who struggled on the bar. You're clearly capable, with that above-average undergrad record showing your academic foundation, yet the model saw your later deciles as red flags based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to the 37-average for passers, or strengthening recent academic showings through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile positions. Admissions teams often review appeals or waitlists, so gathering letters highlighting your full-time commitment and resilience could help reframe your story. Ultimately, many students in similar spots boost their profiles and succeed—focusing on actionable steps like these can shift the odds in your favor for the next cycle. Keep pushing; your GPA proves you have what it takes with the right adjustments.
